summary:
The Nian Rebellion (1851–68) was a peasant uprising in northern China that erupted approximately simultaneously with the [[event:2543|Taiping Rebellion]] in the south and posed a distinct and significant challenge to [[event:2337|Qing dynasty]] authority. Though less ideologically distinctive than the Taiping movement, the Nian rebellion was militarily formidable — its cavalry-based mobile warfare proved extremely difficult to suppress — and it further strained the Qing government's military resources during an already extremely challenging period.

The Nian ('torches' or 'bands') were initially groups of bandit-peasant fighters operating in the border region of Anhui, Henan, Shandong, and Jiangsu provinces — an area devastated by chronic flooding of the Yellow River, opium addiction, and economic disruption from the [[event:2540|Opium Wars]]. The movement coalesced into a serious military force after 1851, adopting mobile cavalry tactics that allowed it to evade and frustrate the Qing armies sent against it.

After the [[event:2624|suppression of the Taiping Rebellion]] in 1864, many surviving Taiping forces merged with the Nian, adding to its strength. The Qing government deployed its most capable commanders — first Zeng Guofan, then Li Hongzhang, and the Mongolian general Sengge Rinchen — against the Nian. Conventional pursuit proved ineffective against the Nian's fast-moving horsemen. Li Hongzhang ultimately succeeded through a strategy of encirclement, building a network of earth walls and moats that progressively restricted the Nian's freedom of movement and cut them off from supplies and recruits.

The Nian Rebellion, together with the Taiping and other concurrent uprisings including the [[event:3636|Dungan Revolt]], devastated large portions of China and killed tens of millions of people. The cumulative effect of these mid-century crises severely damaged the economic and demographic foundations of the Qing state.
